column2: Mechanical bowel preparation versus no bowel preparation for oesophageal cancer surgery: a multicentre, randomized, parallel, open-labelled trial
column33: Non-inferiority was used to calculate the sample size according website http://www.powerandsamplesize.com/ Calculators/. The primary hypothesis to be tested was that the incidence of major complications of NBP was no more than that of MBP. Retrospective studies showed that the major complication rate varied from 30% to 36% in patients undergoing oesophagectomy [17, 18]. Therefore, it was estimated that major complications might occur in 35% of the patients receiving MBP. The margin used to establish the non-inferiority of NBP to MBP was 10%. At a power of 80% and a significance level of 5%, each group required 281 patients; thus, 562 patients should be enrolled. Due to a possible loss of 14% of patients, the final sample size was calculated to be 652 patients.
column37: This study adopted a non-inferiority design to evaluate whether the complication rate of the experimental group (NBP) was non-inferior to that of the control group (MBP). The non- inferiority margin � ¼ 10% was predefined based on acceptable differences in complication rates for oesophagectomy, as established in prior research. Null hypothesis (H 0 ): The complication rate of NBP is at least 6% higher than that of MBP [risk difference (RD) ≤ −10%]. Alternative hypothesis (H 1 ): NBP is non-inferior to MBP (RD > −10%). The analysis employed a 1-sided 97.5% confidence interval (CI) calculated using the Newcombe method. Non-inferiority would be concluded if the upper limit of the RD’s CI did not exceed 6%, thereby rejecting H 0 porting H 1 and sup. To align with recommendations for trials with binary end-points, a relative risk (RR) margin was 1.308 additionally derived from the RD margin and the observed control-group event rate. Null hypothesis (H0): The RR of complications in NBP versus MBP is at least 1.308 (RR ≥ 1.308). Alternative hypothesis (H1): NBP is non-inferior to MBP (RR < 1.308). The analysis employed a 1-sided 97.5% CI for RR calculated using log- transformation. Non-inferiority would be concluded if the upper limit of the RR’s CI did not exceed 1.308, thereby rejecting H0 and supporting H1.

column2: Unilateral subcostal anterior quadratus lumborum block versus intrathecal morphine for postoperative pain in laparoscopic nephrectomy: A randomized controlled trial
column33: We determined that 36 participants per group would provide 80% power (β=0.20) to demonstrate the non-inferiority of the anterior subcostal QL block to ITM on the primary outcome, with a 2.5% significance level. The non-inferiority margin was 5.27 mg (favoring a lower mean), assuming a true mean difference of zero and pooled SD of 7.86. Allowing for 10% attrition, 40 participants per group (80 in total) were planned. The sample size and power calculations were performed using PASS 21 for a non-inferiority test of two means.
column37: We selected the non-inferiority margin (Δ) using the fixed margin approach, informed by previous meta-analyses, a placebo-controlled study, and expert opinion, in accordance with FDA guidance and ICH E10 recommendations.20 21 Based on prior findings, the 24 hours cumulative mean intravenous MME consumption differed between the control (15±8.8 mg) and ITM (2±6.8 mg) groups by 13 mg (95% CI 10.53 to 15.47). Our non-inferiority margin was set at approximately 50% of the lower bound of this CI, yielding 5.27 mg. A pooled SD of 7.86 mg was assumed for morphine consumption distribution.

column2: Multimodal prehabilitation as a strategy to reduce postoperative complications in cardiac surgery: A randomised controlled trial
column33: The sample size was calculated according to the primary outcome of the study. In our previous experience, prehabilitation decreased the incidence of complications in high-risk patients undergoing major abdominal surgery by 51%.8 The incidence of complications in cardiac surgery in our centre is around 30% (unpublished data). Accepting a two-sided alpha risk of 0.05 and a beta risk of 0.20, and anticipating a 10% dropout rate, a sample size of 80 participants per group was calculated to detect a 20% absolute reduction in the percentage of patients suffering complications in the intervention group vs. the control group.

column2: Effect of preoperative exercise on postoperative complications after colorectal cancer surgery in older people with low physical fitness: The CANOPTIPHYS randomised controlled trial
column33: For our main outcome, postoperative complications according to the Clavien-Dindo classification, a standard deviation (SD) of 1.32 was assumed based on a previous observational study exploring the impact of preoperative physical fitness on the risk of postoperative complications in older patients undergoing major abdominal cancer surgery [44]. Based on the assumption that a between group difference of 1 grade is of clinical importance, a significance level of 0.05 and a power of 80 %, 67 participants were considered sufficient to detect a statistically significant difference between groups [27]. As the research project also aimed to evaluate the effect of preoperative exercise on physical fitness, the plan was to recruit 160 participants based on a power calculation for the 6-min walk test. For more information regarding power calculations, see the published study protocol [27].

column2: Hypovolaemic phlebotomy in patients undergoing hepatic resection at higher risk of blood loss (PRICE-2): a randomised controlled trial
column33: PRICE-2 had 80% power to detect a clinically important 10 percentage point decrease in 30 day perioperative red blood cell transfusion incidence (20% in control group vs 10% in hypovolaemic phlebotomy),16 with a two-sided 5% alpha error. A correction factor of 5% for non-compliance in the intervention arm increased the sample size by 11% based on the Lachin formula.23 No adjustment was made for potential losses to follow-up given the short-term perioperative nature of this trial. With our assumptions, the trial planned to recruit to a target of 440 patients who had a liver resection (220 patients per group). This target sample size excluded patients who were randomised but who did not undergo a liver resection (eg, due to disease progression or unresectability), under a modified intention to treat principle.

column2: A multimodal prehabilitation class for Enhanced Recovery After Surgery: a pragmatic randomised type 1 hybrid effectiveness-implementation trial
column25: CCI for all and for those suffering a complication
column33: Our sample size calculation is based on historical site-specific data (Martin 2019). At our institution, LOS is a median 5 [interquartile range, IQR: 4.0] days for colorectal surgery. We previously determined that risk of malnutrition was associated with prolonged LOS (median 6.0 [IQR 5.0] days), in part due to poor adherence to the ERAS care elements. Likewise, preoperative interventions to mitigate malnutrition reduced LOS by 2 days in a meta-analysis of 914 colorectal surgery patients (Gillis 2018). As such, we hypothesized that by participating in the prehabilitation program, preoperative malnutrition and adherence to the ERAS guidelines would improve, thereby reducing LOS. An estimated 200 patients are required to achieve a reduction in LOS by 2 days with a power of 80% and an α-error of 0.05. With an estimated 20% attrition (conservative given reduced follow-up and low participant burden of the trial), we would need to recruit 240 participants.

A sample size of 100 patients per group was estimated to provide a power of 94% to reject the null hypothesis of equal means using a 2-sided, 2-sample equal-variance t test.18,19 According to a meta-analysis, a difference of 10% in %EBMIL resulted in a superior T2D remission rate when comparing gastric banding, RYGB, and biliopancreatic diversion.20 Therefore, a minimal difference of 10% %EBMIL was considered clinically relevant.

column2: The Effect of Perioperative Dexamethasone on Postoperative Complications after Pancreaticoduodenectomy: A Multicenter Randomized Controlled Trial
column33: A recent large trial conducted in 34 French centers (the PACMAN trial) showed that perioperative 0.2 mg/kg dexamethasone reduced morbidities and mortalities by almost 30% in nonthoracic surgeries.14 In addition, the Finnish trial reported that perioperative hydrocortisone reduced major complications after pancreaticoduodenectomy by more than 50% in high-risk patients.17 Therefore, we hypothesized that perioperative dexamethasone could reduce overall complications in the entire cohort by at least 30%. The overall morbidity after pancreaticoduodenectomy, including Clavien-Dindo grades I to V, has been reported to be at least 60%.3,25–27 We chose the CCI as the primary outcome because it is more sensitive and requires a smaller sample size compared with the Clavien-Dindo classification in clinical trials.19 We estimated that the enrolment of 128 patients per arm would provide 80% power with an α-error of 0.05 to detect a 30% relative reduction in the rate of postoperative complications. The final sample size was set at 300 to account for a 20% dropout or protocol deviation.
column46: A recent large trial conducted in 34 French centers (the PACMAN trial) showed that perioperative 0.2 mg/kg dexamethasone reduced morbidities and mortalities by almost 30% in nonthoracic surgeries.14 In addition, the Finnish trial reported that perioperative hydrocortisone reduced major complications after pancreaticoduodenectomy by more than 50% in high-risk patients.17 Therefore, we hypothesized that perioperative dexamethasone could reduce overall complications in the entire cohort by at least 30%. The overall morbidity after pancreaticoduodenectomy, including Clavien-Dindo grades I to V, has been reported to be at least 60%.3,25–27 We chose the CCI as the primary outcome because it is more sensitive and requires a smaller sample size compared with the Clavien-Dindo classification in clinical trials.19 We estimated that the enrolment of 128 patients per arm would provide 80% power with an α-error of 0.05 to detect a 30% relative reduction in the rate of postoperative complications. The final sample size was set at 300 to account for a 20% dropout or protocol deviation.

column2: Morbidity after Mechanical Bowel Preparation and Oral Antibiotics Prior to Rectal Resection: The MOBILE2 Randomized Clinical Trial
column33: The sample size calculation was based on the baseline risk of deep incisional and organ or space SSIs in anterior rectal resections performed at Helsinki University Hospital between 2005 and 2011 (approximately 13%)24 and in our group’s previous trial that recruited patients for colon surgery (the MOBILE1 trial; approximately 6% to 8%).25 The mean (SD) CCIs in the 2 groups in the MOBILE1 trial were 9 (16) points and 10 (13) points. Therefore, we estimated that both the CCI and SD would be higher in patients undergoing rectal surgery than in those undergoing colon surgery. The sample size was calculated with the aim of showing a difference of 5 CCI points between the 2 groups (hypothesis: a mean [SD] 12.5 [18] points in the MOABP group and 17.5 [18] points in the MBP plus placebo group). With a power of 90% and a margin of error of 5%, 574 patients needed to be recruited (Wilcoxon-Mann-Whitney test). About 5% of patients were estimated to be excluded after randomization, resulting in a final sample size goal of 604 patients.19

column2: Robotic versus laparoscopic hepatectomy for liver malignancies (ROC'N'ROLL): a single-centre, randomised, controlled, single-blinded clinical trial
column33: The sample size was based on the hypothesis of a higher QoL after RH within 90 days after surgery as compared to LH. A mean difference of 13% (standard deviation 20) in the role functioning scale was deemed clinically meaningful and used for power calculation in line with previous trials on quality of life after liver surgery.7,28, 29, 30, 31 The approach of sample size calculation based on clinically meaningful differences on the QoL scale was in line with other randomised trials using QoL as primary endpoint.32, 33, 34 A total of 76 patients (38 in each arm) provided 80% power (α = 0.05) to detect this difference with a two-sided significance. column2: Effects of nonintubated thoracoscopic surgery on postoperative neurocognitive function: a randomized controlled trial
column33: The primary outcome was perioperative difference in neurocognitive scores, namely scores on the Qmci-TW test. We calculated the minimum requisite sample size for detecting a difference in Qmci-TW test score for a median score of 5 and an assumed standard deviation of 6 [13, 15] in the treatment group using the method proposed by O’Keeffe et al. [16]. This method prevents overestimates of the sample size because postoperative long-term neurocognitive test is associated with a high dropout rate. Subsequently, a total of 62 patients (31 patients in each group) would provide a power of 0.9 and a two-sided type I error of 0.05. We expected longer-term attrition from loss to follow-up or a withdrawal rate of 35%. Accordingly, a total of 110 participants were enrolled, and we expected to enrol >64 patients to complete the postoperative neurocognitive test at 6 months.

column2: Effect of home-based prehabilitation on postoperative complications after surgery for gastric cancer: randomized clinical trial
column33: The sample size calculation was done using G∗Power 3.1.9.4 software, with reduction in 90-day postoperative complication rates as the primary outcome. Based on the results of a recent RCT8, a 50 per cent reduction in complications (to achieve a 25 per cent complication rate) was predicted for the prehabilitation group. Assuming a drop-out rate of up to 10 per cent, a total of 128 patients (64 per group) were needed to be enrolled in the study.

column2: Postoperative Complications and Mobilization Following Major Abdominal Surgery with Versus Without Fitness Tracker-based Feedback (EXPELLIARMUS): A Student-led Multicenter Randomized Controlled Clinical Trial of the CHIR- Net SIGMA Study Group
column33: The sample size calculation was based on the primary end point “CCI within 30 days after the index operation.” Assumptions were based on the literature:26,27,32 a decrease of 10 points in the CCI was considered relevant by patients and clinicians, and a conservative SD of 20 was assumed. The primary end point was tested simultaneously in the subgroup of patients who underwent minimally invasive surgery and the subgroup of patients who underwent open surgery with an expected ratio of occurrence in patients of 1:1 using a 2-sided t-test. Therefore, the overall 2-sided significance level of α=0.05 was adjusted by Bonferroni correction, yielding α=0.025 for each of the 2 subgroups. Thus, to achieve a power of 80%, a sample size of n=156 (78 per group) had been recruited per subgroup, with a total required sample size of the trial of n=312 (156 per therapy group). To compensate for drop-outs and loss-to-follow-up, another 10% of patients were randomized, leading to a total sample size of n=348 (174 per group).

column2: Drain Versus No Drain in Open Mesh Repair for Incisional Hernia, Results of a Prospective Randomized Controlled Trial
column33: The complication rate in the drainage group was estimated to reach 20%. No prospective study comparing drainage and without drainage was available in the literature when the study was started. Rates of complications were estimated according to a consensus between surgeons participating to the study. We hypothesized that the absence of drainage would decrease the occurrence of either surgical site infection, seroma, hematoma, or early hernia recurrence by 50% within 30 days after surgery. The sample size was calculated with an 80% power; and a significance level with a p-value under 0.05 was established. The sample size calculation according to power yielded 398 patients. Approximately 60 patients per year present an incisional hernia requiring surgery. With a participation rate of 80% among eligible patients, the study was estimated to last over 8 years. A second hospital was included to reduce the study duration. The study was however prematurely interrupted due to the emergence of concurrent minimally invasive techniques and the number of 398 patients was not reached.

According to previous studies, R1 resection rate with standardized analysis of the surgical specimen is around 50% for pancreatic and periampullary malignant tumors.4,13,30 In the few publications on artery-first approach, resection margin invasion is 18% to 27%.14–29 Based on these considerations, a clinically significant reduction of R1 rate from 50% in ST-PD to 25% in AFA-PD was estimated. To achieve a power of 80% and 5% significance level, with a 1:1 ratio of experimental to reference units and considering a 20% drop-out, sample sizes of 72 per arm (144 patients in total) were calculated. The ARCSINUS approach was used for this calculation.

column33: A difference of 10 points on the Comprehensive Complication Index reflects a one-grade difference in the Clavien–Dindo classification, an association based on a previous validation study that assessed the Comprehensive Complication Index as an end point in randomized trials.20 Because infected necrotizing pancreatitis is a heterogeneous disease associated with substantial morbidity, we calculated the sample size by hypothesizing a clinically relevant 15-point reduction in the Comprehensive Complication Index from a mean (±SD) of 40±27 (on the basis of the number of complications observed in previous randomized trials conducted by the Dutch Pancreatitis Study Group) to 25.22,23 Assuming a two-sided alpha level of 5%, a power of 80%, and a 2% loss to follow-up, we calculated the total sample size to be 104 patients.

column33: Based on prospective studies with similar designs, we estimated a postoperative acute urinary retention (AUR) incidence of approximately 11%. Accordingly, we defined an 8% non-inferiority margin, which was considered clinically acceptable. This led to an expected AUR range of 2% to 19%, aligning with previously reported data from authors such as EriksenError! Bookmark not defined., CoyleError! Bookmark not defined., and Stubbs, 32, . With a one-sided alpha risk of 0.05 and a beta risk of 0.2, the required sample size was calculated as 416 patients (208 per group), accounting for a 10% dropout rate. The non-inferiority margin was set at 8% based on clinical and statistical reasoning. Historical rates of acute urinary retention (AUR) after laparoscopic colectomy range between 3% and 10% in the literature. A margin of 8% was considered clinically acceptable, preserving the majority of the comparator's safety profile while allowing for a feasible sample size. This approach aligns with current methodological recommendations, including those by Taylor et al.33, which emphasize that non-inferiority margins should be justified based on preserved clinical effect, biological plausibility, and real-world clinical acceptability

column33: Sample size calculation was done for the subgroup of patients who were to have pancreatoduodenectomy to ensure adequate power for this population. We assumed an expected relative reduction of 50% in the incidence of the primary outcome after pancreatoduodenectomy, on the basis of 13·8% of patients, a two-sided α of 0·05, a power of 80%, an intracluster correlation of 0·009, and a cluster autocorrelation of 1,9,20,22,24 which resulted in a required sample size of 1186 patients having a pancreatoduodenectomy in the 17 centres. The planned study duration was therefore 22 months, on the basis of typical patient numbers per month. The total sample size was expected to be 25% higher than the planned sample size, because all types of pancreatic resection were included. A planned interim analysis was done at 11 months to allow for the study duration to be extended if enrolment was less than 47·5% of the planned sample size.
